title: CHATTER
elevator_pitch: CHATTER is a real-time text surveillance game about drowning in invasive, mostly useless chatter while trying to identify the few dangerous signals hidden inside it. The player works inside a morally deranged authoritarian monitoring system that rewards paranoia, speed, and false certainty.

high_concept.core_fantasy: Sit in front of a live state surveillance interface and make snap decisions about what deserves intervention before it disappears.
high_concept.player_role: A low-level analyst / operator inside an authoritarian internal security apparatus.
high_concept.core_tension: The player is forced to make detention decisions under time pressure using incomplete, ethically contaminated information.
why_it_works: The player cannot meaningfully read everything, so attention becomes the scarce resource.
Most monitored chatter is banal, intimate, embarrassing, or morally indefensible to collect.
A tiny fraction contains real danger, coded behavior, or suspicious contextual shifts.
The system is disgusting, but not entirely delusional, which is what makes it believable.

tone.overall: Claustrophobic, invasive, petty, bureaucratic, ugly, occasionally absurd, and darkly funny.
aesthetic: Brittle system with too much power
tone.moral_frame: The surveillance is supposed to feel obviously ethically bankrupt. The player is not protecting society in a clean heroic sense; they are operating a machine that turns ordinary human mess into suspicion.
humor_style: Occasional surreal nonsense in the chatter pool
tone.guiding_principle: Petty details, maximum confidence, zero understanding.

themes: The state as a pervert engine
The seduction of the 1 percent hit that justifies the other 99 percent
Bureaucratic systems that are both stupid and dangerous

primary_loop: A current hot word is selected from a rotating pool.
Many simultaneous conversations scroll across the screen continuously.
Occurrences of the hot word are highlighted wherever they appear.
The player watches the stream, especially conversations containing the hot word.
The player clicks a conversation to isolate it; other chatter fades toward the background.
Before the selected conversation scrolls off-screen, the player decides whether to detain.
The player repeats this under constant pressure while new chatter continues to arrive.
gameplay.win_state_philosophy: The goal is not to catch everything. The goal is to catch enough of the right things to survive and satisfy the system.
gameplay.failure_philosophy: Missing some true threats should matter, but the game should not demand impossible perfection. It should feel oppressive, not random.

hot_word_system.description: There is a list of uncommon but innocuous words. One is hot at a time on a rotating basis because intelligence believes relevant chatter will cluster around it.
purpose: Direct the player's attention without pretending they can process everything equally
Create visual anchors in a dense text field
Make state paranoia feel organized and arbitrary at the same time
Allow replayable rounds with changing lexical obsessions
word_design_rules: Able to sound literal, metaphorical, logistical, or suspicious depending on context
hot_word_system.design_note: The state chooses what is hot, not the player. This makes the player participate in a moving obsession engine rather than a rational investigation.

chatter_design.core_rule: Most chatter should be ordinary, invasive, useless, or pathetic. A small portion should contain meaningful signal hidden in similar language.
chatter_design.voice_target: Less HBO, more autocorrect. The chatter should feel like texts, side conversations, petty complaints, logistical fragments, and slightly broken everyday language.

signal_to_noise.desired_effect: The player should never fully trust the system, but also never feel safe dismissing it outright.

examples_of_morally_repugnant_surveillan: Private text fragments and inferred behavior

procedural_generation.recommended_approach: Hybrid: keep clean JSON chatter pools, then remix and corrupt them in JavaScript at runtime.
why_runtime_generation: More variety from a smaller source pool
Can bias corruption by category, tension, or hot word
Avoids storing a giant number of premade permutations

host_donor_model.description: Pick a host line, pick a donor line, split the donor roughly in half, and insert one donor half into the host near the middle or at a clause boundary.
host_donor_model.effect: Because the beginning and ending of the host still feel related, the player's pattern-matching brain grants the corrupted message fake coherence.

corruption_guidelines: Preserve punctuation and general style of the host when possible
Clause-boundary splits feel eerie and intentional
Mid-thought splits feel glitchy or intercepted
Dynamic junk is good; authored suspicious lines should remain curated
procedural_generation.design_rule: Generate junk dynamically, author signal deliberately.

ui.visual_concept: An ugly, dense live-monitoring interface with many simultaneous horizontal text channels moving constantly.

layout.main_view: Many stacked horizontal text lanes moving continuously across the screen.
layout.conversation_behavior: Multiple concurrent conversations are present at once; text is always in motion.
layout.highlighting: The current hot word is visually highlighted wherever it appears.
layout.focus_state: When the player clicks one conversation, other conversations mute/fade into a background-near color.

primary_actions: Click a conversation to isolate it
Click detain before the conversation scrolls off

motion.purpose: Create pressure, enforce triage, and make attention feel costly.
motion.note: The motion should feel readable enough to play, but not comfortable.

style_notes: Muted text colors with occasional highlight color for hot words
Should feel cheap and hostile rather than sleek

detain_action.description: A quick irreversible choice made before the targeted conversation scrolls away.

time_pressure.description: The player has limited time to inspect and act before the conversation is lost.
time_pressure.effect: Forces snap judgments and ensures every act of attention neglects something else.

ambiguity.description: The same hot word should appear in harmless, intimate, logistical, sarcastic, metaphorical, and dangerous contexts.
ambiguity.goal: The player is not hunting the word itself, but the use of the word.

pattern_learning.description: Repeated categories, recurring tones, and contextual shifts should help players build ugly heuristics.
examples: A phrase that is harmless in domestic chatter may feel wrong in logistics chatter
A previously normal noun may become suspicious after recent arrests or public pressure
Context clusters matter more than single lines

design_principles: Do not make it about catching everything.
Do make attention the main limited resource.
Do make the surveillance feel invasive and unjustified.
Do hide real signal inside the same sludge as the useless chatter.
Do keep the system morally deranged and institutionally confident.
Do let the player feel the state is both stupid and dangerous.
Do let fake coherence do some of the content generation work.
Do keep the writing flatter and more text-like than prestige-TV dialogue.
player_experience_goals: I cannot read all of this.
I only had time to look at one thing.
The opening and closing of that corrupted message made it feel meaningful even if it wasn't.
I don't trust the machine, but I also don't trust myself.

content_status.notes: The current content base is solid for prototyping, especially with JS remixing. Continued expansion will help reduce repetition during sustained play.

reason_it_fits: Short and ugly in a good way
Focuses on the material being monitored rather than the institution
Feels cold, everyday, and ominous without overselling
